title: Event propagation and delegation

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Event propagation and delegation; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: DOM events travel through capture, target, and bubble phases, with composed paths and default actions distinct from propagation.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: One list listener uses event delegation and closest to handle buttons added later. Your goal is to trace propagation and implement a delegated interaction without duplicate handlers. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Scope, closures and modules; html project site; Create and remove nodes; Method calls and this. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: DOM events travel through capture, target, and bubble phases, with composed paths and default actions distinct from propagation. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Not all events bubble, shadow DOM retargets, passive listeners restrict preventDefault, and stopPropagation does not cancel default action. This draft therefore rejects: preventDefault also stops the event from reaching ancestor listeners.

includes: Handle capture, target and bubble phases and delegate stable ancestor events when appropriate.
Trace propagation and implement a delegated interaction without duplicate handlers.
excludes: Not all events bubble, shadow DOM retargets, passive listeners restrict preventDefault, and stopPropagation does not cancel default action.

outcomes: Trace propagation and implement a delegated interaction without duplicate handlers.

examples.title: Event propagation and delegation: concrete case
examples.situation: One list listener uses event delegation and closest to handle buttons added later.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: One list listener uses event delegation and closest to handle buttons added later.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: preventDefault cancels a cancelable default action; propagation controls are separate.
examples.result: The result follows because preventDefault cancels a cancelable default action; propagation controls are separate.

Step 3 — Reject the misconception: preventDefault also stops the event from reaching ancestor listeners.

analogy.example: A coordinated event desk can model Event propagation and delegation, with values, tasks, callbacks, and external messages changing observable state.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Event propagation and delegation before using exact platform terms
limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.
